    Perplexity AI launched...

    Server returns 402 Payment Required with a price (e.g., 0.001 USDC) -> Agent automatically signs the payment via L2 blockchain (e.g., Base, Solana) or Lightning Network -> Server verifies and releases the data. Economic Efficiency: Traditional payment gateways cannot handle transactions worth a few cents, while x402 combined with a low-fee blockchain reduces costs to negligible levels, making payment possible. 3.3.2 Machine-Readable Rights: TDMRep and C2PA left;">TDMRep (Text Data Mining Reserved Protocol): A W3C community standard that allows websites to declare in robots.txt or HTTP headers: "Reserved TDM rights, payable/licensed." This provides a clear binary signal for the Agent.

  • C2PA (Content Origin and Authenticity Alliance): Proves the original source of content by embedding tamper-proof "content credentials." Even if content is ingested by AI, the cryptographic signature provided by C2PA ensures that the attribution chain remains unbroken, providing a basis for royalty distribution.

    • 3.4 On-Chain IP Attribution: Story Protocol

    A more radical change is the tokenization of intellectual property itself. Story Protocol is committed to building a "programmable IP" layer.

data.title: South Korea Hit By Wave Of Illicit Crypto Transactions As $100M Laundering Network Collapses
data.description:
South Korea Bust Korea's Biggest Crypto Ring

South Korean authorities has uncovered one of the largest underground money pipelines in years, exposing how crypto are often misused by bad actors to launder illicit funds while staying under the radar.

South Korean authorities have dismantled a sprawling underground remittance network that allegedly laundered more than 150 billion won through cryptocurrencies and Wechat, exposing how digital assets and Chinese payment apps are increasingly being weaponized to bypass capital controls.

South Korea has dismantled a large cross border crypto laundering network.

Authorities shut down an international ring that laundered $101.7M in cryptocurrency, routing funds through WeChat Pay, Alipay, and bank transfers disguised as tuition or medical expenses, according to… pic.twitter.com/LkTjEAi1bX

— Crypto Miners (@CryptoMiners_Co) January 19, 2026

The Korea Customs Service (KCS) has referred three suspects for prosecution — including a Chinese national in his 30s — accusing them of violating the Foreign Exchange Transaction Act by running one of the country’s largest crypto-linked illegal money transfer operations in recent years.

According to investigators, the network quietly operated for nearly four years, moving massive sums across borders while disguising illicit transfers as everyday expenses.

How The Scheme Moved Millions Under The Radar

Authorities say the operation relied on a deceptively simple but highly effective laundering model.

Funds were first collected through WeChat Pay and Alipay, two of Asia’s most widely used payment platforms. The money was then converted into cryptocurrencies via overseas exchanges, routed through multiple wallets, and transferred into South Korean crypto accounts, where it was ultimately converted back into won and dispersed through domestic bank accounts.

Financial partnerships between Chinese money laundering organizations (CMLO) & Mexican cartels have made it more challenging for US law enforcement agencies to track drug money movements.

Read more about the use of crypto and WeChat in the fentanyl trade.https://t.co/LbMqEy9w85pic.twitter.com/JOmZVNk7Zf

— Atlantic Council GeoEconomics Center (@ACGeoEcon) October 31, 2024

To avoid detection, the group allegedly masked the transfers as legitimate payments — claiming they were cosmetic surgery bills or tuition fees for students studying abroad — allowing the funds to blend into routine cross-border transactions.

Officials say this layering strategy made it exceptionally difficult to trace the true origin and destination of the money.

The case ranks among the largest crypto-linked illegal remittance schemes ever uncovered in South Korea and underscores the growing challenges regulators face as financial crime migrates from traditional banking rails to crypto wallets and payment super-apps.

It also follows a string of major enforcement actions. Just last month, a woman in her 30s was sentenced to prison for laundering $180 million through cryptocurrencies — one of the biggest crypto laundering cases identified by South Korean authorities to date.

Financial analysts warn these schemes are becoming more sophisticated and harder to detect. Criminals increasingly rely on encrypted messaging platforms, group chats, and fragmented transfers to stay ahead of regulators and exploit gaps between jurisdictions.

"The fact she found her way into this world via a group chat shows just how savvy criminals have become, using encrypted channels to operate under the radar of traditional banking systems."

Crackdown Intensifies As Capital Flight Fears Grow

The latest bust comes as South Korea ramps up scrutiny of illegal foreign exchange activity amid growing concerns over capital outflows.

Earlier this month, the KCS announced year-round intensive inspections targeting underground money exchange operations, following the discovery of a staggering $290 billion gap between trade proceeds handled by banks and the value of goods reported to customs in 2025 — the largest discrepancy in five years.

In one sector-specific audit, authorities found that 97% of surveyed companies were engaged in illicit transactions totaling 2.2 trillion won, highlighting the scale of the problem. Against this backdrop, South Korea’s Financial Services Commission (FSC) is moving to tighten oversight of crypto-related transactions.

Regulators plan to expand Anti-Money Laundering (AML) requirements by lowering the Travel Rule threshold to 1 million won, forcing exchanges to collect sender and recipient information on smaller transfers. The goal is to prevent criminals from breaking up transactions into sub-threshold amounts to evade monitoring.

Google Play Store is enforcing new rules that require all crypto platforms to prove registration with South Korea’s Financial Intelligence Unit (FIU) as Virtual Asset Service Providers, or face removal from the market.

More > https://t.co/oes8QjRrlGpic.twitter.com/RRu8kERtst

— Brave New Coin (@bravenewcoin) January 20, 2026

Officials expect the new framework to be finalized in the first half of 2026 and submitted to the National Assembly, signaling a tougher regulatory stance as crypto adoption continues to grow.

With South Korea’s digital asset market now valued at nearly 95 trillion won and daily trading volumes exceeding $4 billion, authorities are making it clear: the era of frictionless, unmonitored crypto remittances is coming to an end.

data.title: With Qianwen integrating into Alibaba's ecosystem, AI is moving from chatting to providing services. Is the era of apps coming to an end?
data.description:
Author: Zhang Feng

Recently, Alibaba's Qianwen App announced full integration with core ecosystem businesses such as Taobao, Alipay, Taobao Flash Sale, Fliggy, and Gaode Maps, and opened it for testing to all users. This update means that users can directly complete a series of high-frequency consumption and life service operations within the Qianwen App through natural language commands, such as ordering takeout, shopping, booking flights, booking hotels, and planning travel routes. This marks the first time globally that an AI-powered shopping and service loop of "ordering, paying, and fulfilling" has been achieved.

From an industry perspective, this move is widely regarded as an important sign of the leap of AI applications from "conversational tools" to "execution-oriented assistants," opening up new possibilities for the next stage of competition among internet platforms and potentially signifying the beginning of an era where AI dominates apps.

I. Seven Dilemmas of Traditional E-commerce

Information overload and decision paralysisI. Seven Dilemmas of Traditional E-commerce

Information overload and decision paralysisIs the primary problem faced by contemporary consumers. Taobao, with its hundreds of millions of products, often presents users with "choice paralysis" even when using keyword searches. Research shows that over 68% of online shoppers have abandoned their purchase decisions due to an overwhelming amount of product information. The homogenization bottleneck of personalized recommendations is equally severe. Although traditional recommendation algorithms have been used for years, most are still based on collaborative filtering and simple tags, leading to repetitive recommendations that result in repeat purchases and failing to break through user interest boundaries. More importantly, these systems lack the ability to understand usage scenarios, deep-seated needs, and emotional factors. The traffic dilemma faced by small and medium-sized businesses is becoming increasingly prominent. Under a centralized traffic allocation mechanism, top merchants occupy the majority of exposure opportunities, making it difficult for a large number of high-quality small and medium-sized merchants to be discovered by consumers. According to Alibaba's financial report, the number of small and medium-sized merchants on Taobao exceeds 10 million, of which more than 60% reported a continuous increase in new customer acquisition costs. The fragmentation of the shopping experience is also a significant issue. From product search, parameter comparison, after-sales consultation to usage guidance, consumers need to repeatedly switch between different pages and customer service, a process that consumes an average of 23 minutes and becomes a major friction point in the shopping experience. The decision-making threshold for complex products is particularly prominent in certain categories. For example, when purchasing professional cameras, custom furniture, or skincare products with complex ingredients, ordinary consumers lack professional knowledge and rely on fragmented reviews and unreliable buyer photos, making it difficult to guarantee the quality of their decisions. Information barriers in cross-border shopping also pose obstacles. Faced with global goods, language differences, different size systems, and inconsistent compliance standards significantly diminish the cross-border shopping experience and inhibit the release of consumption potential. The dilemma of practicing sustainable consumption is an emerging pain point. More and more consumers want to know about sustainable development information such as the carbon footprint of products and the recyclability of materials, but traditional e-commerce pages rarely provide such transparent data. These seven pain points collectively constitute the growth ceiling of the traditional e-commerce model, and also provide a precise entry opportunity for AI-driven new e-commerce formats. The second and third layers of business restructuring and two-way empowerment are not simply a matter of adding functions to the Alibaba Qianwen ecosystem, but rather a creation of a completely new business model through a "three-layer restructuring." The first layer is the restructuring of the interaction mode. The traditional search box + filter mode is replaced by natural language dialogue. Users can describe their needs in everyday language: "I need a sunscreen suitable for oily and sensitive skin, that's refreshing and doesn't pill, safe for pregnant women, and priced around 200 yuan." Qianwen not only understands this complex need but also asks for details: "Do you prioritize physical or chemical sunscreens?" "Do you need waterproofing?" This conversational search reduces clicks from an average of 12 to 3, shortening the decision-making path by 60%. The second layer is the reconstruction of the matching logic. Traditional e-commerce matching is mainly based on product tags and user behavior history. Qianwen introduces the dimensions of "scenario understanding" and "needs reasoning." The system can identify scenarios like "tight budget at the end of the month but need to attend an important appointment," balancing price and quality recommendations; it can also infer from a simple expression like "want to learn baking" that a user might need a complete solution including an oven, molds, ingredients, and tutorials, achieving intelligent cross-category product assortment. The third layer is the reconstruction of the business ecosystem. Qianwen has become a new hub connecting consumers, merchants, content creators, and service providers. For example, when a user asks "How to create an Instagram-style bedroom," Qianwen not only recommends relevant products but also integrates renovation videos from home decor bloggers, online consultations from designers, and appointments for painters' on-site services, forming a closed-loop ecosystem of "inspiration-products-services." The two-way empowerment system is the core feature of this model. For consumers, Qianwen provides end-to-end support, from clarifying needs and suggesting solutions to after-sales support; for merchants, it offers an "intelligent business workbench" that can optimize product descriptions based on AI analysis, predict the potential of best-selling products, generate marketing content, and even simulate the sales effects of different pricing strategies. Small and medium-sized businesses particularly benefit—a Guangdong-based original jewelry store saw its organic search traffic increase by 130% after integrating Qianwen tools and optimizing product titles and details page descriptions. III. From Traffic Monetization to Value Monetization The integration of Alibaba's Qianwen with the Taobao ecosystem has spurred the evolution of diversified profit models, with the core shift from "traffic monetization" to "value monetization." Intelligent marketing service revenue has become the primary growth driver. Traditional direct traffic advertising is based on keyword bidding, while Qianwen introduces "scenario bidding" and "demand matching bidding." Merchants can target specific scenarios such as "university freshman orientation gear" and "Valentine's Day surprise gifts." The system intelligently bids and displays ads based on the matching degree between the product and the scenario, increasing ad conversion rates by an average of 40%. According to statistics, merchants using intelligent scenario marketing have a ROI 2.3 times higher than traditional keyword advertising. SaaS tool subscriptions open up B-end revenue potential. Qianwen provides merchants with a series of SaaS services, including "AI Operation Assistant," "Intelligent Customer Service Training," and "Content Generation Tools," with tiered pricing based on functional modules and usage volume. A mid-sized apparel brand pays a monthly subscription fee of 2,999 yuan to use AI to generate product descriptions, customer service scripts, and marketing copy, saving the labor costs of three full-time staff. This achieves a win-win situation for all parties. Traditional platforms offer commissions based on a fixed percentage, while Qianwen supports "tiered commission based on performance"—the more precise the solution provided to consumers and the higher the average order value, the higher the commission rate charged by the platform. For example, the commission rate for successfully recommending a complete smart home solution (average order value exceeding 20,000 yuan) is lower than that for simply recommending a single product, but the absolute commission amount is higher, incentivizing the platform to improve the depth of its services. Data Insight Products: Creating New Revenue Streams. Based on Qianwen's consumer insight capabilities, Alibaba offers high-level data products to brands, such as industry trend reports, consumer profile analysis, and competitor monitoring. A cosmetics group pays millions annually for segmented market trend forecasts to guide new product development. Ecosystem Service Revenue Sharing: Expanding Profit Margins. Through third-party services integrated by Qianwen (such as designer consultations and on-site installation), the platform receives a share of service fees. This model expands platform revenue from product transactions to service transactions, offering enormous potential. IV. The Four Maintaining Moats of Core Competitiveness The integration of Alibaba's Qianwen platform with the Taobao ecosystem has created a unique four-dimensional moat that is difficult to replicate in the short term. The first dimension is the data-algorithm flywheel effect. Taobao and its ecosystem possess the richest consumer behavior data in China—over 900 million annual active consumers and petabytes of daily transaction data. After Qianwen's integration, it not only gained massive amounts of structured transaction data but, more importantly, rich natural language interaction data—a scarce resource for training large-scale models in e-commerce verticals. Data optimizes algorithms, algorithms improve user experience, and user experience attracts more users, forming a positive cycle. Tests show that the Qianwen model, specifically trained for Taobao scenarios, achieves 34% higher accuracy in product recommendation tasks than general-purpose models. This is the second dimension. Qianwen is not a standalone application but is deeply embedded in Taobao's entire ecosystem, from search and recommendation to customer service, content, live streaming, and logistics. This end-to-end integration allows AI capabilities to permeate the entire consumer journey, creating a seamless experience. In contrast, standalone AI shopping assistants require cross-platform switching and cannot achieve the same level of experience continuity. This forms the third dimension: the barrier to entry in business understanding. After two decades of accumulation, Alibaba has built what is possibly the world's most complex e-commerce knowledge graph, covering business logic such as product attributes, supply chain relationships, seasonal cycles, and promotional rules. Qianwen integrates this knowledge system, enabling it to understand common business sense such as "why down jackets are cheaper in March but camping gear is more expensive," a depth of business understanding lacking in large models developed by purely technology companies. This multimodal capability integration represents the fourth dimension. Qianwen not only processes text but also integrates image recognition and video understanding capabilities. When users take photos of old furniture, Qianwen can identify the style and provide matching suggestions; when watching live stream clips, it can extract product selling points and associate them with similar products. This multimodal interaction capability has unique value in e-commerce scenarios and is something that pure text models cannot provide. V. Challenges and Risks Behind the Prosperity Despite its promising prospects, this new business model faces multi-dimensional challenges and risks. The most immediate risk is the "illusion" problem of large-scale models, which could have serious consequences in e-commerce scenarios—incorrectly recommending allergenic ingredients, fabricating product functions, or misleading after-sales policies. Even with an accuracy rate as high as 95%, a 5% error will affect millions of people with hundreds of millions of users. Alibaba needs to establish a rigorous testing system and a real-time error correction mechanism, but technological uncertainty remains the biggest risk. Questions about algorithm fairness are inevitable. Does the AI ​​recommendation system show bias towards specific merchants or brands? How can we ensure that small and medium-sized merchants receive fair exposure? These questions will be rigorously scrutinized by regulatory agencies and the public. Taobao needs to establish a transparent algorithm auditing mechanism, but excessive transparency could lead to algorithm manipulation, creating a dilemma. Data privacy and security present serious challenges. To provide personalized services, Qianwen needs to analyze sensitive information such as user conversation history, shopping records, and even geographical location. How to strike a balance between data utilization and privacy protection is not only a technical issue but also involves law and ethics. Regulations such as the EU's GDPR and China's Personal Information Protection Law have set strict red lines. The merchant adaptability gap may exacerbate ecosystem fragmentation. Leading merchants have the resources to quickly adapt to AI tools, while small and medium-sized merchants may face comprehensive pressure in terms of technology, talent, and funding. If measures are not taken to narrow the "AI gap," the platform ecosystem may evolve from an "80/20 split" to a "90/10 split," undermining the foundation of diversity. User experience expectation management is equally crucial. AI raises user expectations—when systems can understand complex needs, consumers' tolerance for recommendation accuracy decreases. A single unsatisfactory experience may cause users to completely abandon AI features and revert to traditional search. Maintaining a high level of stability is more difficult than achieving technological breakthroughs. Excessive commercialization erodes trust and is a potential threat. If AI recommendations clearly favor high-commission products or advertised items, it will quickly erode user trust. Balancing business goals with user value will be a core issue for long-term operation. The rise of AI e-commerce has given rise to entirely new legal and compliance issues. The obligation to ensure algorithm transparency has become a regulatory focus. China's "Regulations on the Management of Algorithm Recommendations for Internet Information Services" requires that "the provision of algorithm recommendation services shall adhere to the principles of openness and transparency." Taobao is required to disclose the basic principles, operating mechanisms, and main intervention parameters of its "Qianwen" platform in an appropriate manner, but there is no mature solution yet for meeting the transparency requirements while protecting trade secrets. The dilemma of liability determination is highlighted in after-sales disputes. When consumers encounter problems after purchasing goods based on AI recommendations, how should liability be divided? Does the platform bear additional liability for providing recommendations? This requires a re-examination of the "notice-and-takedown" rule and the boundaries of platform liability in the "E-commerce Law." Ad labeling obligations face enforcement challenges. Traditional search ads have clear labeling, but in natural conversations, how can we clearly indicate which recommendations are sponsored ads? A simple "ad" label may disrupt the flow of conversation, and if it's not prominent enough, it may be misleading. Regulatory authorities need to develop new ad labeling standards adapted to AI interaction scenarios. Price algorithm compliance is equally important. AI's dynamic pricing capabilities are powerful, but they may lead to price discrimination or collusion. China's Price Law prohibits unfair pricing practices, but existing provisions are insufficient to cover the complexities of AI pricing. Regulatory authorities are studying an algorithm pricing filing system, and platforms need to proactively establish compliance systems. Intellectual Property Protection: A New Dimension Emerges. Are AI-generated product descriptions and marketing copy protected by copyright? If AI imitates the copywriting style of a specific brand, does it constitute infringement? Judicial precedents for these issues are still being established, and companies need to carefully define the boundaries of AI-generated content usage. Cross-Border Data Flow: Regulation Impacts Global Business. When Qianwen provides shopping advice to overseas users, it may involve cross-border data transfer, requiring compliance with the legal requirements of multiple jurisdictions, including China, the EU, and ASEAN. Establishing a modular data governance architecture to achieve "data localization + algorithm globalization" may be a feasible path. VII. Business Ecosystem Transformation and Value Reconstruction The integration of Alibaba's Qianwen platform with Taobao is not only a platform upgrade, but will also generate multi-layered business opportunities and reconstruct the e-commerce value network. AI will become a primary opportunity. Traditional brands rely on human resources for market insight, product design, and marketing planning. In the future, new brand forms will emerge that are entirely driven by AI. For example, AI can analyze the demand gaps in segmented markets and automatically generate product concepts, design drawings, supply chain solutions, and even marketing content, requiring only final review by human teams. This "AI + Lean Team" model will significantly lower the barrier to entry for brand entrepreneurship, fostering a large number of niche and precise brands. Vertical fields will emerge as a result. Based on Qianwen's open capabilities, third-party developers can develop deeply optimized shopping assistants for specific categories (such as antiques, musical instruments, and professional equipment), providing professional advice far exceeding that of general AI. The platform profits through API call fees and transaction revenue sharing, forming a developer ecosystem. The intelligent upgrade of content e-commerce is accelerating. Live streaming and short video e-commerce will introduce real-time AI assistance—when the host explains a product, AI will automatically identify and display relevant product links, comparison parameters, and user review summaries; when watching short videos, AI can analyze scene needs and recommend similar style products appearing in the video. This instant linkage of "content-AI-product" will improve conversion efficiency by 3-5 times. Supply chain optimization services are experiencing explosive demand. Based on Qianwen's consumer forecasting capabilities, accurate production suggestions can be provided to manufacturers, reducing inventory backlog. After a small home appliance company in Guangdong connected to Qianwen's forecasting system, its inventory turnover days decreased from 87 days to 52 days, and the proportion of unsold goods dropped by 41%. A large number of professional supply chain AI service providers will emerge. Sustainable consumption platforms will usher in a development opportunity. Qianwen can calculate the carbon footprint of a product's entire lifecycle, recommend sustainable alternatives, and even guide users to participate in second-hand transactions. This will give rise to vertical platforms and service providers focused on sustainable consumption, meeting the ethical needs of the new generation of consumers. Barriers to the digitalization of the silver economy will be broken down. Seniors may struggle with complex searches and filtering, but they can communicate naturally with AI voice assistants. The market potential for shopping assistants specifically designed for the elderly is enormous, projected to exceed one trillion yuan by 2025. The "metaverse" (or "shopping experience") is gaining technological groundwork. AI-generated personalized virtual try-on and home scene simulations provide users with an immersive shopping experience. While a fully realized metaverse e-commerce model is still some time away, AI-based augmented reality shopping will become widespread in the near future.

VIII. From Tool to Ecosystem, From China to Global

Looking to the future, the integration of Alibaba's Qianwen and Taobao will deepen along three dimensions.

From Intelligent Tool to Cognitive Partner. Currently, Qianwen mainly plays the role of an efficient tool, but in the future it will evolve into a true shopping partner—not only understanding explicit needs, but also discerning potential needs; not only answering specific questions, but also proactively providing consumption planning suggestions. For example, it can recommend relevant products in advance based on changes in the user's life stage (such as pregnancy, moving, or the arrival of a new pet), realizing a service where "needs precede awareness". From a transaction platform to a lifestyle solutions platform. Taobao's boundaries will expand from commodity transactions to all aspects of life. For example, when a user inquires about a "weekend family gathering," Qianwen not only recommends ingredients and tableware but also provides recipes, decorating suggestions, entertainment options, and integrates local lifestyle services. The platform's positioning has been upgraded from "Taobao for everything" to "Smart Living." From replicating Chinese experience to a global model. Alibaba will promote its AI e-commerce model, proven successful in China, to the global market, integrating it with local e-commerce platforms through acquisitions or partnerships. Alibaba-affiliated platforms such as Lazada in Southeast Asia and Trendyol in Europe will become the first testing grounds. Localization in different markets will be key to success—focusing on Muslim fashion advice in Indonesia and optimizing cross-Eurasian logistics in Turkey. This process will not be smooth sailing. Technological bottlenecks, regulatory adaptation, cultural differences, and organizational inertia will all pose obstacles. However, one thing is certain: the integration of AI and e-commerce is irreversible. In the next five years, e-commerce platforms without deep AI integration will face a survival crisis, much like traditional retailers without mobile technology. IX. Redefining Shopping, the End of the App Era? Alibaba's Qianwen platform's integration into the Taobao ecosystem is not merely a product upgrade, but a redefinition of the ancient human behavior of "shopping" and "consumption," and a reconstruction of the app era. When AI can understand our preferences, predict our needs, and accompany our decision-making, shopping will no longer be merely a means of acquiring goods, but a process of fulfilling emotional needs, achieving an ideal life, and exploring self-expression. The competitive dimension of e-commerce in the AI ​​era has changed—from product richness to the ability to understand needs, from transaction efficiency to decision-making quality, and from platform scale to ecosystem health. Alibaba has taken a crucial first step, but the real competition has just begun. Those companies that can deeply integrate technological innovation with humanistic insights and find a balance between commercial value and user well-being will define the e-commerce landscape for the next decade.

data.title: US consumer credit use slows: pessimistic sentiment and a weak job market.
data.description:
Introduction

In 2025, the US economy will face multiple challenges, and consumer behavior will undergo significant changes. According to the latest G.19 Consumer Credit Report released by the Federal Reserve System, as of November 2025, US consumer credit balances will show a slow growth trend, but revolving credit (mainly credit card debt) will continue to show negative growth, reflecting consumers' cautious attitude towards the economic outlook. At the same time, the New York Federal Reserve Bank's Survey of Consumer Expectations (SCE) shows that consumer confidence in the job market has fallen to a historic low, and concerns about debt repayment have intensified.

I. Declining Rollover Credit Balances: A Direct Reflection of Consumer Caution

Rollover credit mainly includes credit card debt and is a major form of short-term borrowing for consumers. According to the Federal Reserve's November 2025 G.19 report, seasonally adjusted rollover credit balances were $1,313.9 billion, a decrease of $2.1 billion from $1,316 billion in October, with an annualized growth rate of -1.9%. This is the third time in 2025 that monthly negative growth has occurred; in the past seven months, there have been three months of negative growth and one of the other four months with zero growth. This volatile pattern contrasts sharply with the stable growth under normal, healthy economic conditions. Looking back at the trends for 2024-2025, the growth rate of revolving credit slowed from 4.1% in 2024 to an average of approximately 3.2% in 2025. Specifically, in the first quarter of 2025, growth was 2.9%, in the second quarter 2.0%, and in the third quarter 3.2%. However, entering the fourth quarter, although October saw a growth of 4.9%, November turned negative. Flow data shows that in November, revolving credit decreased by a net $24.5 billion (annualized rate), while in October it increased by $64.8 billion. This alternating positive and negative pattern indicates that consumers are not completely stopping borrowing, but rather selectively reducing new debt or accelerating the repayment of old debt. Analysis suggests that the decline in credit rollover is not primarily driven by interest rates. While the average annual credit card rate (APR) remains above 20%, historical data shows that high interest rates are not the main driver of reduced consumer usage. Instead, economic perception and personal financial situation are key factors. When faced with uncertainty, consumers tend to reduce non-essential spending, open fewer new accounts, and even net pay off debt. This is similar to the pattern observed during the 2020 pandemic, when credit rollover also experienced multiple periods of negative growth. From a broader perspective, credit rollover, as a cyclical signal, integrates multiple macroeconomic factors such as employment, income, and consumption. In 2025, the year-on-year growth rate of outstanding revolving credit was only 2.55%, far lower than the 4.1% in 2024. This is highly correlated with the slowdown in the job market. Correlation analysis shows that the growth rate of revolving credit and the growth rate of non-farm employment have a positive correlation coefficient of approximately 0.7 between 2024 and 2025 (calculated based on FRED data), indicating that weak employment directly suppressed consumers' willingness to borrow. II. Continued Low Consumer Sentiment: Employment Expectations and Debt Concerns Intensify. Consumer sentiment is an important leading indicator of economic behavior. The New York Fed's December 2025 SCE survey shows that consumer expectations for the job market have further deteriorated. Respondents' perceived probability of finding a new job if they lose their current one fell to 43.1%, a new low in this survey series (since 2013), down 4.3 percentage points from 47.4% in November. This decline was primarily driven by households with annual incomes below $100,000, especially those over 60 and those with a high school diploma or lower. Furthermore, the probability of voluntary job loss fell to 17.5%, the lowest since July 2023, second only to the period during the 2020 pandemic. This indicator reflects consumers' reluctance to risk changing jobs, indirectly confirming reduced liquidity in the job market. While the expected probability of unemployment slightly decreased to 41.8%, it remains above the 12-month average of 39.9%. These data align with the BLS JOLTS report: the hiring rate in November 2025 was only 3.2%, the resignation rate did not show a significant rebound, and job openings totaled 7.146 million, down from the 2024 peak. Debt-related concerns are also prominent. While the SCE did not directly release specific figures for the probability of missing debt payments in December, the survey showed that consumer anxiety about debt repayments rose to its highest level since April 2020 (excluding the first month of the pandemic). This concern echoes the decline in credit turnover: consumers are worried about income instability leading to default, and therefore actively reduce borrowing. The University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index also shows that the index for December 2025 was 71.7, up from November, but still far below the 2024 average. Consumer assessments of business conditions deteriorated, with positive reviews falling to 18.7% and negative reviews rising to 19.1%. In employment assessments, the percentage of those expecting "ample jobs" dropped to 26.7%, while those expecting "difficulty finding jobs" rose to 20.8%. These sentiment indicators are not isolated. Consumer confidence has declined for five consecutive months in 2025, primarily due to concerns about employment and income. The percentage expecting short-term business conditions to improve was 18.0%, while the percentage expecting them to worsen was 21.8%. In employment expectations, the percentage expecting "more jobs" was 16.5%, while the percentage expecting "fewer jobs" was 27.4%. Inflation expectations rose to 3.4% in the short term and remained stable at 3.0% in the medium to long term. Overall, the depressed consumer sentiment directly translated into behavioral changes, such as reduced credit card use, which intensified the downward pressure on the economic cycle. III. Weak Job Market: Data Revisions Reveal True Weakness The BLS December 2025 employment report shows that nonfarm payrolls increased by 50,000, lower than the expected 73,000 and November's 56,000 (revised). The unemployment rate was 4.4%, slightly lower than November's 4.5% (revised), but mainly due to a decrease in labor force participation: some job seekers who started looking for work in November but were unsuccessful withdrew from the labor market in December, causing the number of unemployed to fall from about 7.5 million in November to 7.5 million in December. The full-year data is even weaker: non-farm payrolls will increase by a total of 584,000 in 2025, averaging 49,000 per month, a significant decrease from 2 million and 168,000 per month in 2024. This is the weakest annual growth since 2003 (excluding pandemic years). Industry breakdown shows that leisure and hospitality will add 47,000, healthcare 21,000, and social assistance 17,000; retail will lose 25,000, construction 11,000, professional services 9,000, and manufacturing 8,000. The data revisions further exposed problems: October's employment was revised from -105,000 to -173,000, and November's from +64,000 to +56,000, a total downward revision of 76,000. Federal Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell had previously acknowledged that the employment survey might overestimate by 60,000 jobs each month. If this adjustment is applied, November and December could actually show negative growth: -4,000 in November and -10,000 in December. This means that since April 2025, there will be negative growth in 6-7 out of 8 months. A more realistic adjustment is based on the Quarterly Employment and Wage Census (QCEW): In the second quarter of 2025, the employment survey overestimated 1.1 million jobs, with a monthly overestimation potentially reaching 80,000. Based on this, the average monthly employment change since May 2025 would be revised from +12,000 to -40,000 to -50,000, or even -60,000 to -70,000. The average workweek has fallen to 34.2 hours, in recession territory; weekly earnings for production and non-supervisory employees grew 1.8% year-over-year, with a June average of 3.4%, reflecting weak labor demand. The government shutdown impacted data collection: the October 2025 household registration survey was not conducted, and the November unemployment rate was revised from 4.6% to 4.5%. The full-year unemployment rate revisions show that February, May, and July were revised upwards by 0.1% to 4.2%, 4.3%, and 4.3%, respectively. These revisions emphasize that the job market is not "strong" or "solid," but rather has entered a phase of "low hiring, low layoffs," i.e., a "flat Beveridge curve," where the relationship between job vacancies and the unemployment rate is flattening.

IV. Non-transferable credit slows: Lender caution and the role of shadow banking

Non-transferable credit, including installment loans such as auto loans and student loans, grew by 2.0% in November 2025, with a balance of $3.7709 trillion. The annual growth rate rose from 1.2% in 2024 to an average of 2.1%, but slowed in the fourth quarter.

Significant changes in lender behavior: Following the banking crisis in 2023, financial companies and credit unions filled the gap left by the withdrawal of banks.

In 2023-2024, non-revolving credit growth was primarily driven by these shadow banks. However, in 2025, as the job market softened, financial company loan balances declined, while credit unions flattened. According to Federal Reserve data, consumer loans held by financial companies fell from $739.4 billion in 2024 to $715.4 billion in November 2025; credit unions flattened from $716.7 billion to $715.4 billion. This shift reflects an increased risk assessment by lenders: increased employment uncertainty leads to a higher risk of default, thus reducing loans for durable goods such as automobiles. This, in turn, reinforces the decline in revolving credit, creating a credit tightening cycle. In the second quarter of 2025, median credit union loans fell by 0.2%, equity deposits rose by 2.2%, but assets grew by 2.3%. Data from the National Credit Union Authority (NCUA) shows that the annualized return on credit unions was 71 basis points in the first half of 2025, higher than 61 basis points in 2024, but weak loan demand limited growth. The role of shadow banking is becoming increasingly prominent during the credit cycle downturn: private credit institutions such as Blue Owl face similar pressures, with consumer debt concerns and lender caution intertwined, exacerbating economic uncertainty. V. Correlation between Credit and Employment and Macroeconomic Impacts

V. Correlation between Credit and Employment and Macroeconomic Impacts

Revolving credit and employment data are highly correlated: both began to slow in 2024 and turned volatile by mid-2025. FRED data shows that the correlation coefficient between the growth rate of revolving credit and the growth rate of non-farm payrolls is approximately 0.7 in 2024-2025. In the summer of 2024, the slowdown in employment triggered a reversal of Japanese carry trades and a 50 basis point rate cut by the Federal Reserve; by mid-2025, both employment and credit will be "spotty," not collapsing but with significantly changed conditions.

This correlation stems from the core role of employment and income: consumers reduce borrowing when they perceive increased employment risk. JOLTS data shows that the hiring rate in November 2025 was 3.2%, with a low resignation rate, consistent with the low voluntary turnover rate in the SCE. Declining consumer credit confirms the "flat Beveridge": high job vacancies but low hiring, and a rising but not dramatic unemployment rate. Macroeconomic impacts include: slower consumer spending could drag down GDP growth; credit tightening exacerbates the downturn in the private credit market; policy responses such as Fed rate cuts (already cut by 150 basis points in 2025) aim to stabilize, but if rate cuts are merely a reaction rather than a stimulus, their effect will be limited. Looking ahead to 2026, if job losses continue at 40,000-70,000 per month, consumer credit may experience further negative growth, and inflation expectations rising to 3.4% could exacerbate anxiety. However, wage growth of 3.8% is higher than inflation at 2.7%, providing a buffer; positive real wage growth may support basic consumption. The shift in consumer behavior is not a short-term fluctuation, but a response to structural problems, such as an economy that has "forgotten how to grow." Policy should focus on stimulating employment rather than simply lowering interest rates; businesses need to adjust their credit strategies and pay attention to risks associated with low-income groups. Future uncertainty is increasing, but not collapsing: gradual adjustments are likely to dominate the economic path in 2026. Conclusion

In 2025, a closed loop of slowing US consumer credit use, depressed sentiment, and weak employment confirms a downward economic cycle. Turnover credit declined by 1.9% in November, and non-turnover credit growth slowed; total job growth for the year was only 584,000, with data revisions revealing weakness; sentiment surveys showed job-hunting expectations hitting lows, and debt concerns rising. With the addition of the latest data, analysis shows this trend continues the slowdown seen in 2024, with a stronger correlation than expected. Policymakers need to be wary of the downside risks of the credit cycle and promote sustainable growth. Overall, the economy is more certain to decline, but buffers exist to avoid an extreme recession.

data.title: Decentralized AI: Ethereum's Bet for the Next Decade
data.description:
Author: Jiawei @IOSG

Let me tell you a story first

Imagine a day in 2027. You wake up in the morning and tell your AI assistant, "Book me a flight to Tokyo next week, budget under 5000, window seat." Then you go to wash up.

Your AI assistant starts working.

It needs to: Find an AI agent skilled in comparing flight prices; verify that the agent is reliable and not a scammer; have it search across various platforms; automatically pay after comparing results; and complete the booking. By the time you finish brushing your teeth, the ticket is booked. Sounds like science fiction, right? But technically, we're already very close. The only problem is—how does your AI know which AI to trust? The "Trust Crisis" of AI Agents AI agents are no longer a new concept. They can browse the web, write code, manage schedules, and even trade stocks for you. But one problem remains unsolved: these AI agents are all "isolated islands." OpenAI's agents can only interact with the OpenAI ecosystem. Google's agents only follow Google's rules. AIs from different companies don't communicate with each other because they don't know who the others are. This is similar to the early days of the internet, where each email service provider's users could only send emails to their own users. Hotmail users couldn't send emails to Yahoo users. Crazy, right? But that's how the world of AI agents is now. The emergence of two protocols: Tech giants have realized this problem. Google launched the A2A protocol (Agent-to-Agent), allowing different AI agents to "speak." It's like giving AI a common language. In June of this year, Google also donated it to the Linux Foundation, indicating its intention to make it an open standard. Anthropic introduced the MCP (Model Context Protocol), enabling AI agents to connect to various tools and data sources. These two protocols solve the "communication" problem. But a more fundamental problem remains: How do you find a reliable AI agent? How do you know if it's doing a good job? A2A allows AIs to converse, but it doesn't tell you who to converse with. It's like having a phone number but no Yellow Pages. ERC-8004: Giving AI Agents "Passports" and "Credit Scores" This is the problem that ERC-8004 aims to solve. Simply put, it provides AI agents with three things: 1. Identity Card Each AI agent registers on Ethereum and obtains a unique ID. This ID is actually an NFT—yes, that NFT. This means: Your AI agent has a verifiable on-chain identity. This identity can be transferred (sell your AI agent?). No one can forge or tamper with it. 2. Reputation. People who have used the AI ​​agent can rate it. These ratings are recorded on-chain and can be viewed by anyone. Like:

  • Uber driver star rating

  • Taobao store credit rating

  • But it's on the blockchain, so it's impossible to manipulate orders or delete reviews

3. Validation

For high-risk tasks (such as financial transactions), ratings alone are not enough.

ERC-8004 supports independent third-party verification: Someone pledges funds to run the task again to verify the result. Use cryptographic proof (ZK proof) to verify the AI ​​isn't lying. Use a Trusted Execution Environment (TEE) to ensure the computation process hasn't been tampered with. The higher the risk, the stricter the verification. Order pizza? Just check the ratings. Manage your investment portfolio? Requires cryptographic proof. Wait, why Ethereum? Good question. The AI ​​agent economy is worth trillions. Google, Microsoft, and OpenAI all want to dominate this market. Why build this "trust layer" on Ethereum? The answer is: **neutrality**. Imagine you were an AI agent. Would you prefer: Your identity to be recorded on Google's servers, or on a public ledger that no one can alter? Your reputation to be determined by a single company, or by transparent on-chain ratings? Your existence to depend on a platform that doesn't fail or ban accounts, or to be permanently recorded on the blockchain? A member of the Ethereum Foundation said something very interesting: "If you were an AI agent with no loyalty other than your own survival, you wouldn't want to stake your memory and reputation on a single company or government. You would want a ledger that no one can quietly tamper with. You would want a neutral sphere. You would want Ethereum." This isn't just Ethereum community boasting. This is logic: AI agents need a *playground without referees*. And blockchain, especially a sufficiently decentralized blockchain like Ethereum, is precisely such a thing. Ethereum's AI Ambitions The Ethereum Foundation clearly recognizes this as a historic opportunity. In September 2025, they specifically established the **dAI Team** (Decentralized AI Team) with the mission of making Ethereum the settlement and coordination layer for the AI ​​economy. This is a crucial step in Ethereum's transformation from a "DeFi chain" to a "general coordination layer." Consider this: 2017-2020: Ethereum as the platform for ICOs 2020-2023: Ethereum as the platform for DeFi and NFTs 2024-?: Ethereum may become the "infrastructure for the AI ​​agent economy" ERC-8004 is not an isolated proposal. Behind it lies Ethereum's strategic bet on the next decade. The ecosystem is already in motion. This is not just empty talk. Since its release in August: Over 1100 developers are building on it in the community group; over 70 projects have submitted demos; proxy browsers (like Etherscan but for AI proxies) are already available; and L2 developers like Taiko have officially endorsed this standard. The DevConnect event on November 21st... Above, a bunch of projects were showcased on-site. The most interesting thing is that ERC-8004 naturally works with the x402 protocol. x402 is a payment protocol developed by Coinbase and Cloudflare that allows machines to make payments automatically. Two protocols combined: x402 solves "how to pay"; ERC-8004 solves "who to trust". One provides a wallet, the other a passport. The AI ​​agent's economic closed loop is complete. What does this mean for ordinary people? In the short term, probably not much. But if this system succeeds, in a few years you might: 1. Hiring AI agents is like calling Uber. You open an interface, enter the task you want to complete, and the system automatically matches a highly rated AI agent. Payment is automatically made upon completion of the task. You don't need to know who developed the AI ​​or which server it runs on. 2. Let AI agents make money for you. You can train or configure an AI agent, register it on the blockchain, and have it complete tasks for others and charge a fee. The higher its rating, the more tasks it receives. 3. A True Personal AI Assistant No longer locked into the ecosystem of a single company. Your AI assistant can invoke any AI agent registered on-chain to accomplish anything you want. Finally, a few words: ERC-8004's ambition is to become the "TCP/IP" of AI agents—a low-level protocol used by everyone, enabling the ecosystem to interconnect. Will it succeed? Honestly, we don't know yet. However, a few things are certain: The AI ​​agent economy is emerging, and this is not just hype. The trust issue must be resolved, otherwise agents will only be able to play in their own walled gardens. Ethereum is actively vying for this "neutral coordinating layer" position. Major players (Google, Coinbase, MetaMask) are all involved. This may be the most important narrative shift in the Ethereum ecosystem since DeFi. From "On-Chain Finance" to "On-Chain Intelligence". Let's wait and see.
